body_txt: A character’s choice of drugs reflects their behavior and motivations, serving as a reflection of their character. Sometimes, the reason why a character takes a drug is sidelined or left to speculation, with the act itself used mainly to emphasize the tragedy, carelessness, or villainy of the character who partakes in them. Although drugs are often treated as having the same narrative effect, their drug of choice reflects their personality. Crafting drugs shows how intelligent a character is , as it requires skills in chemistry to know how to make it safely and guarantee how the drugs will affect the user. It shows guile and stealthiness because, to administer it to a character, they have to estimate the weight of the user and how to mask the smell of the drug so that it will be undetected. To get past security or guards in a non-violent way , they use drugs to knock them out . For villains, it shows their sadism because they carefully plan out what drugs will do to people and how to administer them tactically and in an undetectable way . Drug abuse shows that a character is uncaring or apathetic, as they think the drugs are harmless and won't kill anyone. When administered to someone else, it shows that they don't see their target as a human being; they have no regard for safety, consent, or well-being . For someone who uses drugs indiscriminately, this behavior suggests a pattern of reckless overindulgence and highlights how dangerously carefree they are , living hedonistically. For athletes and sportstars, it shows they are desperate or forced to cheat to win. A subset of this trope is the pecking order between different users. Stoners distance themselves from harder drug users. With stimulants, snorting or freebasing cocaine is seen as high-class, but casual and intense, respectively, while amphetamine is seen as low-class, but all others look down on crack users. Among opiate users, those abusing prescription medicine consider themselves distant from heroin users, while the use of codeine cough syrup (aka Lean) is seen as above the others, a luxury, and not "really" abusing opiates. This is about the reasons why a person would partake in drugs, not the justification or condemnation of their usage. A character's reasons for taking drugs can vary from curiosity, desperation, entertainment, and survival.          This is a Super-Trope to: If the drugs lead to a rampage, it can go with Drug-Crazed Savagery . When addressing the ugliness of addiction, it can go with Fiending for a Fix . It can go with Drugs Are Bad , as it can be a means of establishing a lesson about the dangers of irresponsible drug usage. It can also go with Fantastic Drug when fictional drugs are used as examples. Contrast The Teetotaler , Straight Edge Evil , and Smug Straight Edge , who are characterized by not using drugs.
